topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I watched Liam laugh with Sarah across the crowded cafe, a sound that used to pull a sharp, familiar ache in my chest, but now just registered as background noise. He turned, his eyes sweeping the room, and for a fraction of a second, they landed on me. There was no spark, no flicker of recognition, just a brief, blank sweep before he was absorbed back into his conversation. I traced the rim of my mug with my thumb, the ceramic cool against my skin. It felt like watching a poorly dubbed film—the plot was there, the actors were present, but the connection was entirely missing. I took a slow sip, letting the bitterness settle, and decided that perhaps this absence was just a fact, nothing more substantial than a minor inconvenience in my day.

[story 2]

I leaned against the brick wall, the evening air sharp against my jacket. When Liam finally approached, his smile was broad, the kind he always reserved for people he genuinely enjoyed. "Hey," he said, his voice casual. I nodded, a small, automatic movement. He started talking about his new project, his enthusiasm infectious, and I offered brief affirmations—'Right,' 'That sounds interesting.' Inside, a quiet settling occurred, a sort of release of tension. It wasn't sadness, not exactly; it was more like watching a distant ship sail away. I noticed the way his gaze drifted over my shoulder, lingering for a moment, then moving on. It was an observation, nothing more. I shrugged, the movement barely disturbing my posture, and focused on the distant streetlights, letting the feeling wash over me without resistance.

[story 3]

The memory of our shared summer adventures felt thin, like old, faded photographs. I sat on the park bench, watching couples walk by. Liam walked up, a familiar shadow appearing in my periphery. He stopped a few feet away, offering a greeting that was practiced and polite. "Long time," he managed, a slight hesitation in his tone. I met his eyes, and there was a neutrality there, a flat surface reflecting nothing back. I didn't offer a reciprocal smile. I simply watched him, noting the slight furrow in his brow as he tried to place me. When he didn't seem to connect, I shifted my weight, pulling my hands into my pockets. There was no surge of hurt, only a quiet acknowledgment that the expected warmth simply wasn't there, and that was perfectly acceptable.
